findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify or specify the actual snapshot writer that produces `max_loc`/`over_cap_count` records (or clarify that this ticket must also add a snapshot-write step to populate those fields), since no existing writer emits them.
evidence: scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py lines 112-118: record = {'complexity': ..., 'clone_count': ..., 'source': ..., 'confidence': ...} — no max_loc or over_cap_count
scripts/harvest_gha.py writes coverage_pct and red_to_green_recovery records only

Search for 'over_cap_count' in scripts/ returns zero hits
snapshot.py has no scc reference
findings.finding: The plan's per-metric semantics for `module_size_trend` assert 'Input = stored per-snapshot `max_loc`/`over_cap_count` records (written by the scc-based snapshot path)'. A search of all snapshot-writing code (backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py, harvest_gha.py) reveals that no existing snapshot writer emits `max_loc` or `over_cap_count` fields — the backfill script writes `{complexity, clone_count, source, confidence}`. There is no 'scc-based snapshot path' writing these fields anywhere in the codebase.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics — `module_size_trend`: 'Input = stored per-snapshot `max_loc`/`over_cap_count` records (written by the scc-based snapshot path)'
scenarios: Executor implements module_size_trend to read max_loc/over_cap_count from snapshots but the snapshot store never contains those fields, so the metric always returns Unavailable even on the rebar repo

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (or reference the ticket that specifies) the forward writer that appends `{max_loc, over_cap_count}` snapshots to `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`; without it, `module_size_trend` will always be Unavailable for new data after migration.
evidence: 'module_size_trend: reads the TIME SERIES from the existing snapshot store … Input = stored per-snapshot max_loc/over_cap_count records (written by the scc-based snapshot path)'
backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py record shape: {"complexity": int, "clone_count": int, "source": ..., "confidence": ...} — no max_loc, no over_cap_count keys anywhere in the codebase
No ticket in the dep chain (3b30, 18f9, 21fb, 3ba0) specifies writing max_loc/over_cap_count snapshots
findings.finding: The `module_size_trend` data-flow has a forward-writer gap: the plan specifies a reader that consumes per-snapshot `max_loc`/`over_cap_count` records 'written by the scc-based snapshot path', but no writer of those keys is identified or specified in this ticket or any dep. The ONLY existing snapshot writer (`backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py`) writes `{complexity, clone_count}` records — no `max_loc` or `over_cap_count` keys. Without a specified forward writer, `module_size_trend` will always be Unavailable post-migration for all new data points, making the metric permanently dead on arrival.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics — `module_size_trend`
scenarios: After migration, rebar metrics is run on the in-repo project; `module_size_trend` finds snapshots but none have `max_loc` or `over_cap_count` keys; the reader produces Unavailable or incorrect output forever because no writer ever populates those keys.
A foreign repo runs rebar metrics for the first time; the snapshot store is empty and remains empty because no forward writer is wired to append LOC snapshots on each run.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step (or a dep ticket reference) that wires the scc LOC adapter (18f9) to write a snapshot record containing `{max_loc, over_cap_count, ts}` after each successful analyzer run — either as a post-compute hook in the `module_size_trend` compute function itself, or as a named harvest script analogous to `harvest_gha.py`.

findings.finding: The module_size_trend time-window boundary case — snapshots exist in the store but none fall within [since, until] — is a distinct failure path for newly introduced logic, but the testing plan only specifies a 'foreign repo -> Unavailable' regression (no analyzer at all). This leaves the out-of-range-snapshots case unverified, which is a separate production scenario where the function must also return Unavailable rather than a fabricated or empty-but-authoritative value.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria: 'Unavailable when no snapshots in range' / Verification section
scenarios: User queries module_size_trend for a date range with no historical snapshots (e.g., a fresh repo that started capturing metrics yesterday queried over last year) — the time-window filter returns empty but the foreign-repo regression test would still pass because it never exercises this code path
findings.suggested_fix: In test_git_metrics.py, add a test that seeds the mock snapshot store with entries timestamped outside the query window and asserts the result is Unavailable / matches the no-data contract, distinct from the 'no analyzer' regression test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Canonically state the required shape of `AnalyzerResult.loc` that this plan's per-file computation (`over_cap_count`, `near_cap_count`) depends on (e.g. `dict[str, int]` mapping file path to LOC), and confirm that 18f9's scc adapter will populate it at that granularity.

engine_b_backends._scc_files extracts per-file dicts with a 'Code' key from scc JSON — this is the data that would need to be in AnalyzerResult.loc for per-file counting to work
findings.finding: The `module_size_distribution`/`oversized_module_count` mechanism assumes `AnalyzerResult.loc` carries per-file LOC counts, but this type contract is never established in this plan or in any dep. The current implementation iterates individual files and counts their lines; replicating `over_cap_count` (files strictly exceeding the cap) requires per-file granularity. If `AnalyzerResult.loc` is an aggregate (e.g. total LOC across the repo or per-language sum), the per-file count is uncomputable, silently breaking the metric.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics — `module_size_distribution`, `oversized_module_count`
scenarios: Implementer interprets AnalyzerResult.loc as an int (total LOC) — cannot compute over_cap_count; returns 0 silently or crashes.
Implementer interprets AnalyzerResult.loc as a dict of filename→int — works, but this shape is incompatible with how 18f9 describes it ('per-language LOC + max_loc').
findings.suggested_fix: In the Per-metric semantics section, add: 'Requires AnalyzerResult.loc to be a mapping of file-path → LOC (e.g. dict[str, int]); if 18f9 populates it differently, the computation step must include a normalization layer.'

scenarios: Implementer skips the opt-in path; there is no failing test. Alternatively, implementer implements it but it's untested and silently broken.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback procedure for cap_change_events deregistration (e.g., 'restore spec from commit X by re-adding registry entry Y; no data migration required') or document an expand-contract phase (mark deprecated before removal in a prior release).
evidence: 'DECISION: REMOVE it from the portable code_health registry (deregister the spec)'
'State the removal explicitly in the changelog note' — a changelog entry is not a rollback path
No expand-contract phase or back-out procedure is described anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The removal of cap_change_events from the portable registry is a breaking change to the public metric set with no rollback path or expand-contract sequence described. The plan acknowledges the removal and justifies it, but T4 requires an explicit back-out mechanism — acknowledgement alone is insufficient per the rubric.
findings.location: Description — cap_change_events DECISION; Acceptance criteria AC2
scenarios: A downstream consumer (CI dashboard, external metrics consumer) is silently broken when cap_change_events disappears from the registry response; there is no way to revert without re-registering the spec manually.
If the removal turns out to be premature (e.g., rebar-only opt-in not yet ready), there is no documented procedure to restore the spec.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line rollback note: 'To revert: re-register cap_change_events spec from commit <sha> — no schema or data migration required since the metric was a stub reading a file path.' This satisfies T4's reversibility requirement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the analyzer-source migration decision and cap_change_events registry removal in a durable artifact (ADR, AGENTS.md section, or design-doc update) so future contributors understand the portable-registry contract and don't re-introduce file-scan coupling.
evidence: 'DECISION: REMOVE it from the portable code_health registry (deregister the spec). It may survive as a rebar-only opt-in guarded on the file existing, but it is NOT part of the client-facing analyzer stack. State the removal explicitly in the changelog note.'
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc capture anywhere in the plan.
This is a cross-component contract change (portable registry surface area reduced) that future contributors need to discover and understand.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a significant architectural decision (pivoting code_health metric readers from direct file-scan to analyzer abstraction + snapshot store, and deregistering cap_change_events from the portable registry) but only mentions a 'changelog note' for the removal — no ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc update is planned to capture the rationale and the new contract for downstream consumers or future contributors.
findings.location: Description / 'cap_change_events' decision / 'Files likely touched' section
scenarios: A future contributor adds a new metric reader and unknowingly re-introduces file-scan coupling because the rationale for the migration is only in a transient changelog note, not a durable design artifact.
A downstream consumer queries cap_change_events and gets silence; the registry change is undiscoverable without git archaeology.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short ADR or AGENTS.md section titled 'code_health metrics source of truth' stating: (1) LOC/complexity/duplication signals flow from the analyzer adapter, not direct file scans; (2) cap_change_events is rebar-governance-only and excluded from the portable registry; (3) missing analyzer → Unavailable is the specified contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (and test) the error-handling path for I/O failures on the snapshot store: distinguish parse/permission errors from data-absence, emit a structured log/warning on error, and confirm the metric renders Unavailable (not a fabricated value) with an internal trace — not just a silent swallow.
evidence: 'module_size_trend: … Unavailable when no snapshots in range' — specifies data-absence, not I/O error.
'NO leaked errno reason' — guards consumer-visible output but leaves the internal error handling path unspecified.
ABSENCE: no mention of logging, exception handling strategy, or instrumentation for snapshot-store read failures.
findings.finding: The plan specifies graceful Unavailable for data-absence conditions (no snapshots in range, no analyzer configured) but does not address how actual I/O errors on the snapshot store (corrupted NDJSON, permission denied, partial read) are handled or surfaced — the 'NO leaked errno reason' AC guards against raw errno leaking to metric consumers but does not specify a recovery/logging path for genuine read failures.
findings.location: AC line 3 ('No analyzer configured -> Unavailable … NO leaked errno reason') / 'module_size_trend' per-metric semantics
scenarios: The snapshot NDJSON is partially written (e.g., interrupted previous run), causing a parse error mid-iteration; the metric silently returns Unavailable with no log, making the failure invisible to operators.
A permission error on `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` is swallowed, indistinguishable from 'no data' to both the consumer and the operator.
findings.suggested_fix: In the snapshot-read wrapper, catch `OSError`/parse exceptions separately from empty-result, log a structured warning (e.g., `logger.warning('snapshot read failed: %s', exc)`) and return Unavailable with a reason tag (e.g., `reason='snapshot_read_error'`) distinct from `reason='no_data_in_range'`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py` (delivered by 3b30) is landed before this ticket begins; or gate the plan explicitly on 3b30's completion status rather than asserting the file already exists.
evidence: Directory listing of src/rebar/metrics shows no analyzer.py
Ticket 3b30 status: 'open'
3b30 plan-review manifest entry: 'dep absent src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py'
3b30 plan-review manifest entry: 'dep absent tests/unit/metrics/test_analyzer.py'
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'analyzer.py is created by 3b30 (recorded depends_on edge) — reference, do not re-create; adapters live in `analyzers/`' treating `src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py` as an existing artifact this ticket can import. In fact ticket 3b30 is still open, and its own plan-review manifest certifies 'dep absent src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py'. The file does not exist in the repo. This ticket cannot import or reference an artifact that has not yet landed.
findings.location: Review-driven corrections — 'analyzer.py is created by 3b30 (recorded depends_on edge) — reference, do not re-create'
scenarios: Executor tries to import from rebar.metrics.analyzer and gets ImportError because 3b30 has not shipped
If 3b30 and this ticket land out of order, module_size_distribution/oversized_module_count compute functions have no analyzer to call
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'analyzer.py is created by 3b30 — reference, do not re-create' with 'This ticket MUST land after 3b30 closes; verify `src/rebar/metrics/analyzer.py` is present before starting implementation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale explaining why routing `module_size_distribution`/`oversized_module_count` through the analyzer abstraction (rather than parameterizing the existing file-walk with a config-injected cap) is the chosen approach — e.g. portability to non-Python repos, future language-agnostic expansion, or consistent Unavailable contract across all code_health metrics.
evidence: No rationale is stated for why the analyzer abstraction (vs. direct file scan + config cap) is the right fit for module_size_distribution/oversized_module_count
The named problem is 'drop hardcoded src/rebar + module-size cap file' — both achievable by parameterizing the existing scanner without the analyzer layer
The analyzer layer introduces new risk: AnalyzerResult.loc type contract, scc tool availability, forward-writer gap for module_size_trend
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for routing `module_size_distribution`/`oversized_module_count` through the analyzer abstraction rather than the simpler alternative of keeping the existing direct file-walk and injecting `size_cap` from the `[code_health]` config. A structurally different alternative — config-inject the cap into the existing scanner, skip the analyzer indirection for these two metrics — achieves the stated goals (portable, no hardcoded limit file, configurable cap) with lower blast-radius and no dependency on `AnalyzerResult.loc` type correctness, and is not obviously inferior.
findings.location: Description / Approach
scenarios: Implementer wires the analyzer and discovers AnalyzerResult.loc is an aggregate, breaking over_cap_count; the simpler alternative (parameterized scanner) would have avoided this entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm with parent (839b) that `cap_change_events` deregistration is authorized: either update the parent's plan to explicitly include this removal (which re-scopes 839b and requires its re-review), or narrow the leaf to only remove the hardcoded `.github/module-size-limit.txt` reference and keep the spec as an optional/rebar-only policy as the parent's language implies.
evidence: Parent (839b) AC: 'No hardcoded `src/rebar` / `.github/module-size-limit.txt`; scan root + size policy are per-project config.' — targets the hardcoded path, not the metric spec itself.
Parent (839b) AC: 'Module-size cap re-expressed as optional policy over the LOC signal.' — says re-express, not deregister.

Parent (839b) description: 'Per-project config selects tool + thresholds; the rebar module-size CAP becomes one optional policy layered on the generic LOC signal (not hardcoded).' — again, re-express as policy, not remove from registry.
Leaf (0734) adds: 'DECISION: REMOVE it from the portable code_health registry (deregister the spec).' — this removal of a registered spec is not found anywhere in parent 839b's plan.
findings.finding: The leaf deregisters `cap_change_events` from the portable registry — a deliverable the parent's plan does not contain or authorize. The parent (839b) only says to remove hardcoded references to `.github/module-size-limit.txt` and re-express the size cap as an optional policy; it never says to remove a registered metric spec. The leaf may be going further than what the parent sanctions.
findings.location: Description > cap_change_events bullet; AC line 2: '`cap_change_events` spec deregistered from the portable registry (documented)'
scenarios: Implementer removes cap_change_events from the portable registry acting on the leaf's authority, but reviewers of the parent later find no parent-level authorization for the removal and flag a divergent implementation.
A client relying on the cap_change_events metric (even minimally) finds it silently gone after this ticket ships, with no parent-level change-log requirement having triggered downstream notice.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit AC to the parent (839b): 'Remove `cap_change_events` from the portable registry; it may survive as a rebar-only opt-in.' Only then does the leaf's deregistration decision become an authorized subset of the parent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either update parent (839b) AC 1 to carve out snapshot-backed metrics ('trend metrics read from the snapshot store, not the live analyzer'), or add a clarifying note in this leaf that explains how module_size_trend still satisfies the parent's portability intent (it reads scc-produced snapshots — the same tool).
evidence: Parent (839b) AC: '`code_health` metrics computed via the analyzer stack (scc/lizard/jscpd) for a Python client AND at least one non-Python client.' — no carve-out for trend.

Leaf (0734) Description: '`module_size_trend`: reads the TIME SERIES from the existing snapshot store (`.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` via `rebar.metrics.snapshot.read_snapshots`), NOT a single scan.' — explicit NOT-analyzer path.
Leaf (0734) adds rationale (stored records written by scc-based snapshot path) but parent plan does not authorize this divergence.
findings.finding: The leaf states `module_size_trend` is read from the snapshot store (not the analyzer), but the parent's AC says 'code_health metrics computed via the analyzer stack (scc/lizard/jscpd)' without carving out an exception for trend data. The leaf's approach is architecturally motivated (trend requires a time series), but the parent's AC as written covers all code_health metrics including trend.
findings.location: Description > module_size_trend bullet; parent (839b) AC line 1
scenarios: Reviewer auditing against the parent's AC finds that module_size_trend is NOT computed via the analyzer stack, which literally fails the parent's AC — even though the leaf's approach is architecturally reasonable for a time-series metric.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to parent (839b) AC: 'Trend metrics (`module_size_trend`) read from the scc-produced snapshot store rather than a live analyzer invocation — this satisfies the portability AC because the snapshot data is written by the analyzer stack.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (or add) a `'metrics'` entry in `_optional.py`'s `EXTRAS` closed vocabulary before dep ticket 21fb's `guard_import(extra='metrics')` call is executed; alternatively, align dep 21fb to use an existing extra key (e.g. `'grounding'`) or catch `ValueError` in addition to `OptionalDependencyError`.
evidence: Ticket 21fb description: 'lizard is used AS AN IMPORTED LIBRARY here (via `guard_import(extra="metrics")`)'
src/rebar/_optional.py line 73: 'if extra not in EXTRAS: raise ValueError(f"unknown extra {extra!r} (known: {', '.join(sorted(EXTRAS))})")'
src/rebar/_optional.py EXTRAS dict (lines 32-45): only 'agents', 'eval', 'tracing', 'grounding' — no 'metrics' entry
AC: 'No analyzer configured -> Unavailable; regression test asserts NO {count:0,max_loc:0} authoritative value and NO leaked errno reason'
findings.finding: The plan's Unavailable-on-absent-lizard path (via dep 21fb) asserts `guard_import(extra='metrics')` will produce an OptionalDependencyError catchable to return Unavailable, but `_optional.py`'s EXTRAS dict is a closed vocabulary with no 'metrics' key — calling `guard_import(..., extra='metrics')` will instead raise a `ValueError('unknown extra "metrics"')`, which is NOT caught by the lizard adapter's except clause, causing a crash rather than the promised Unavailable sentinel. This plan's AC3 ('No analyzer configured -> Unavailable') depends on this guard working correctly.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics / Review-driven corrections ('Cap value comes from [code_health] config e7a0'); dep ticket 21fb ('lizard is used AS AN IMPORTED LIBRARY via guard_import(extra="metrics")')
scenarios: Executor implements lizard_complexity.py calling guard_import('lizard', extra='metrics') per dep ticket 21fb's spec; at runtime this raises ValueError (unknown extra) rather than OptionalDependencyError, the except clause does not catch it, and the metric reader crashes instead of returning Unavailable.
CI runs the regression test for 'no analyzer -> Unavailable' — the test fails because the adapter crashes on import rather than returning a clean Unavailable sentinel.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `'metrics': ('lizard', 'code-health metric tools (lizard complexity, scc LOC)')` to the `EXTRAS` dict in `src/rebar/_optional.py`, and ensure `pyproject.toml` defines a `[metrics]` optional-dependencies section listing `lizard`. Then dep ticket 21fb's `guard_import('lizard', extra='metrics')` will behave as specified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve and state the exact fate of `cap_change_events`: either (a) fully remove it (no conditional opt-in) with a single clear rule, or (b) specify the opt-in mechanism concretely (which registry, when registered, what the updated test should assert) so the executor has an unambiguous decision rule.
evidence: 'It may survive as a rebar-only opt-in guarded on the file existing, but it is NOT part of the client-facing analyzer stack.' — plan prose
No AC line governs the rebar-only survival path; AC line 2 only covers 'deregistered from the portable registry (documented)'

The existing tests (`test_git_metrics_heldout.py:61`, `test_git_metrics_heldout.py:98`) import and assert `cap_change_events` in REGISTRY with source='git' — the plan says update these tests but gives no spec for what the updated assertions should be for the opt-in path
findings.finding: The `cap_change_events` 'may survive as a rebar-only opt-in guarded on the file existing' clause introduces an underspecified conditional decision rule: no protocol is stated for when or how this conditional opt-in activates, what its registry registration path looks like, or who is responsible for this in the current ticket vs. a later one.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics / cap_change_events
scenarios: Executor deregisters cap_change_events from the portable registry but leaves it conditionally registered when `.github/module-size-limit.txt` exists — the test update then becomes ambiguous: should `cap_change_events` appear in REGISTRY for a rebar repo (where the file exists) or not?

findings.finding: `cap_change_events` is a git-history reader (git log -p over .github/module-size-limit.txt to find historical cap value changes), but the plan's replacement stack — analyzer (point-in-time scc/lizard invocation) + [code_health] config (static key) — has no historical dimension. The plan says 'cap_change_events reads via the analyzer / config' (AC1) and 're-express' (Review-driven corrections) but provides zero mechanism for how a git log of file changes is re-expressed through a point-in-time tool. As written, the implementing engineer has no specified output for this metric post-migration: suppress it, make it unconditionally Unavailable, or something else.
findings.location: AC1 / Review-driven corrections: 'BOTH limit-file readers must be migrated: module_size_distribution AND cap_change_events'
findings.suggested_fix: In AC1, split the clause: 'module_size_distribution, oversized_module_count, module_size_trend read via the analyzer / config; cap_change_events is retired to Unavailable (the limit file it tracked no longer exists as a governance artifact; accruing_since preserved).' Remove cap_change_events from the 'reads via analyzer' obligation.

findings.finding: `module_size_trend` is specified to be wired to the analyzer, but the analyzer (scc via dep 18f9) is a single-point-in-time LOC snapshot — it has no temporal series. A trend metric requires at least two time points. The plan provides no mechanism for how a single-invocation analyzer result produces a trend value, leaving the implementing engineer without a workable data-flow.
findings.location: Review-driven corrections: 'module_size_trend / oversized_module_count are currently SEED stubs … this ticket WIRES them to the analyzer'

findings.finding: `cap_change_events` is specified to be 're-expressed via the analyzer / config', but `AnalyzerResult` (defined in 3b30 with fields loc/complexity/duplication) has no field for git-commit history. The plan gives no alternative data source or computation protocol for what `cap_change_events` should return after migration.
findings.location: AC line 1 / Review-driven corrections ('BOTH limit-file readers must be migrated: … cap_change_events') / git_metrics.py:167-229

findings.finding: `module_size_trend` is described as a seed stub to be 'wired to the analyzer', but the plan specifies no computation protocol: it is unclear whether this is a point-in-time LOC value, a time-series delta, or a rolling aggregate, and which `AnalyzerResult.loc` subfield(s) supply the input.
findings.location: Review-driven corrections ('module_size_trend … this ticket WIRES them to the analyzer') / registry.py:135-141

findings.finding: The plan requires `cap_change_events` to 'read via the analyzer / config' (AC line 1), but the analyzer protocol (3b30 fields: loc/complexity/duplication) carries no git-commit-history signal, and once `.github/module-size-limit.txt` is dropped there is no file to git-log over. Implementing 'cap_change_events via the analyzer' as written would require either (a) silently making it always-Unavailable (undocumented), or (b) re-expressing it as git-log over a TOML config file — rebuilding git-scanning under a new abstraction. The plan does not resolve which path is intended, creating a risk of over-engineering or silent metric loss.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria, AC line 1: 'cap_change_events read via the analyzer / config'
findings.suggested_fix: If `cap_change_events` has no equivalent signal once the flat cap file is gone, declare it retired in the AC: 'cap_change_events deregistered from REGISTRY and removed; no stub left in place.' This is the simplest path and avoids over-engineering a config-history tracker.

findings.finding: The plan asserts cap_change_events will be 're-expressed via the analyzer / config' (AC: 'cap_change_events read via the analyzer / config'), but no feasible mechanism is specified. cap_change_events currently tracks git commits that changed a git-tracked file (.github/module-size-limit.txt). When the cap source moves to a [code_health] config key, there is no analogous git-tracked artifact to diff — the analyzer LOC signal and the current config value carry no historical change record. The plan does not name whether this metric becomes Unavailable permanently, is retired, or uses a different history source.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — 'cap_change_events read via the analyzer / config'; Description — 'Re-express the module-size cap as an OPTIONAL policy over the analyzer LOC signal'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Description or AC: 'cap_change_events is retired (unregistered from REGISTRY) when the limit file is removed; no history-of-config-changes is tracked. The metric returns Unavailable with reason="cap_change_events retired: cap is now a static config policy" when called.' This avoids the capability gap entirely and is consistent with the plan's goal of killing fabricated-zeros mode.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify in the plan what REGISTRY source label module_size_distribution carries after migration (structural vs git) and confirm test_git_metrics_heldout.py:98 is updated to remove both cap_change_events AND adjust the module_size_distribution source='git' filter accordingly.
evidence: test_git_metrics_heldout.py:98: named = {"module_size_distribution", "churn", "cap_change_events", "refactor_to_addition_ratio"}
test_git_metrics_heldout.py:93: git_specs = {s.id: s for s in REGISTRY if s.source == 'git'}
registry.py:136-141: module_size_trend seeded as source='structural'; module_size_distribution currently registered by git_metrics.py as source='git'

Plan says 'git-delta (churn/refactor_ratio) + agent_process metrics unchanged; existing git_metrics tests updated to match' — does not explicitly specify that the module_size_distribution REGISTRY label changes from source='git' to a new value
findings.finding: test_git_metrics_heldout.py line 98 asserts `named = {"module_size_distribution", "churn", "cap_change_events", "refactor_to_addition_ratio"}` are present in REGISTRY entries with source='git'. After this ticket (a) deregisters cap_change_events and (b) migrates module_size_distribution off the git scan to the analyzer (which per registry.py seeds it as source='structural'), this assertion fails on two counts — both the cap_change_events membership check AND the module_size_distribution source='git' check. The plan names the file for update but does not call out that the REGISTRY membership assertion for module_size_distribution must also change.
findings.location: tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics_heldout.py:92-100 (test_git_metrics_registered_with_labels) / AC line 4
scenarios: Executor updates the file to remove cap_change_events from the named set but leaves module_size_distribution — the test still fails because module_size_distribution is no longer in git_specs (source='git' filter).
Or the executor leaves source='git' on module_size_distribution after migration, which contradicts the registry.py seed pattern where structural metrics use source='structural'.
findings.suggested_fix: In 'Review-driven corrections', add: 'module_size_distribution (and oversized_module_count) register with source="structural" after migration; update test_git_metrics_heldout.py test_git_metrics_registered_with_labels to remove cap_change_events from named set and to check structural-sourced specs rather than git-sourced ones for the module-size metrics.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
